column3:

column9: Very convenient if I get there early enough to find a spot!  I am new to EVs as of this spring. After purchasing, I drove to campus more frequently because it was the most convenient place to charge. Now that I know more of the chargers around town, I plan to return to walking most of the time and driving only if needed because of schedule or urgent charging requirement (eg because of upcoming travel).  One note: I don’t understand the pricing at all. It claims to be free for the first two hours but isn’t. There doesn’t seem to be a rate change at the 2-hour mark even though I get a notification that there is. I’ve used ChargePoint elsewhere (eg Williams College) and it’s been actually free. Williams also had a waitlist feature if the chargers were full. TL;DR: get more chargers, clarify pricing, consider waitlist feature.

column9:
The main problem with using Campus EV charging stations is that you really need to move your vehicle when it's done charging, which will be in the middle of the day when finding nearby parking is impossible or difficult. 

For full time staff, taking a break in the middle of the day to move ones car around, especially timing it around meetings and obligations, is a barrier.  I understand the reason for charging cars that are fully charged, because of limited availability, but that also feels like a tax one pays to not interrupt their work day.

That being said, a large EV bank in the Garage could go a long way to improving the situation.  People who complain about parking often are complaining about the process of finding parking, because they are trying to seek the perfect spot.  Ones frustration with parking is mitigated if there is a solid place you go every day, like the Garage, so encouraging the use of the Garage can reduce parking stress, period, and if you need to move your EV because it is done charging you know you'll have a spot nearby.

In fact, I would say that reducing the total number of lots and increasing large parking areas like the Garage would actually make people happier (which they wouldn't admit during the process), and give more green space for students to enjoy.
